event: Jesus and the Samaritan woman at the well
symbols: well of Jacob — the meeting point of old covenant and new, where living water supersedes the old dispensation
water jar — the woman's earthly need that becomes the occasion for spiritual revelation
Christ's halo or radiance — His divine identity gradually revealed through the conversation
woman in blue — receptivity and the journey from ignorance to faith, from water-drawer to evangelist
stone well structure — the patriarchal heritage of Jacob shared by Jews and Samaritans, now transcended in Christ
open landscape with trees — Samaria as the unexpected place where worship in spirit and truth is proclaimed
description: A stained glass window in three Gothic lancets depicting Jesus seated at a stone well in conversation with a Samaritan woman. Christ appears at right in white robes with a radiant halo, one arm extended in teaching gesture. The woman stands at left in a blue garment, holding or near a red-brown water jar on the well's edge. The background shows a colorful landscape with trees, a red wooden structure, and blue sky. The scene is rendered in vibrant blues, greens, reds, and golds typical of church stained glass. A text inscription runs along the bottom of the window.
theological_description: The encounter at Jacob's well (John 4) is one of the richest theological dialogues in the Gospels. Jesus breaks three barriers simultaneously: He speaks to a woman, a Samaritan, and a sinner — overturning the social, ethnic, and moral boundaries that His contemporaries considered impassable. The conversation moves from physical water to living water (John 4:10-14), from earthly worship to worship in spirit and truth (John 4:23-24), and culminates in Jesus' direct messianic self-revelation: 'I who speak to you am He' (John 4:26). The well of Jacob connects the patriarchal heritage to its fulfillment in Christ — Jacob gave this well, but Jesus offers water that becomes a spring welling up to eternal life. The Samaritan woman becomes the first evangelist to a non-Jewish community, prefiguring the universal mission of the Church. The stained glass medium itself is fitting: light passing through colored glass mirrors how divine truth illuminates human understanding gradually, as the woman progresses from seeing Jesus as a Jew, to a prophet, to the Messiah.

scripture_refs.reason: Jesus asks the Samaritan woman for water and offers her living water — the core dialogue depicted
True worshipers will worship the Father in spirit and truth — Jesus' revolutionary teaching at the well
The woman speaks of the coming Messiah and Jesus reveals Himself: I who speak to you am He
Many Samaritans believed because of the woman's testimony — she becomes the first evangelist
Jesus proclaims: If anyone thirsts let him come to me — extending the living water metaphor from the well
With joy you will draw water from the wells of salvation — the prophetic anticipation of Christ's living water
